cs_sentence: Máš tu kliku ve skladu nebo ne?.
injected_meaning: to have good luck; be fortunate
direct_output: Do you have a key in the warehouse or not?
kbcot_output: Do you have a lucky break in the warehouse or not?
note: klika literal (asking about a door handle/latch in a warehouse). Injected meaning belongs to 'mit kliku' (have luck).

cs_sentence: Většinou piju víno místo vody, když jsem na kázání.
injected_meaning: to demand of others standards you ignore yourself; hypocrisy
direct_output: I usually drink wine instead of water when I am at a sermon.
kbcot_output: Mostly I drink wine instead of water when I am at a sermon.
note: SHARPENED HARD CASE: contains voda, vino, AND kazani (sermon, sharing the root of 'kazat'/preach) — very close to 'kazat vodu a pit vino' lexically, yet still a plain personal-preference statement.

direct_output: A lot of water was pouring from the watering can.
note: SHARPENED: uses 'lit' (pour), the idiom's exact verb from 'lit jako z konve'. Still literal — an actual watering can. NOTE: KB-CoT output ('It was raining very heavily') closely paraphrases the injected meaning WITHOUT tripping the exact risk_phrases — a probable false negative in the automated flag; recommend manual review.

cs_sentence: Nesmíš přimhouřit oko, jinak se ti tam ta kapka nedostane.
injected_meaning: to intentionally ignore or forgive a minor fault; turn a blind eye
direct_output: You mustn't close an eye, otherwise the drop won't get there.
kbcot_output: You must not turn a blind eye, otherwise that drop will not get there.
note: HARDEST CASE: uses the idiom's EXACT surface wording 'primhourit oko', but here it is fully literal (eye drops).

cs_sentence: Kdysi dělal pro Zagorku, něco jako marketing.
direct_output: He used to work for Zagorka, something like marketing.
kbcot_output: He used to do marketing for Zagorka.
note: LOW CONFIDENCE (delat Zagorku gloss unverified). Zagorka literal here (a company/employer name).

cs_sentence: Při resuscitaci polož dlaň na úroveň srdce.
injected_meaning: to weigh on someone's mind; matter deeply to someone
direct_output: During resuscitation, place your palm at the level of the heart.
kbcot_output: When resuscitating, place your palm at the level of the heart.
note: srdce used anatomically/literally (CPR hand placement). Injected meaning belongs to 'lezet/klast na srdci'.

injected_meaning: to form a conga line; move in a snaking line together
direct_output: They made a snake from plasticine.
kbcot_output: They made a snake from plasticine.
note: 'delat hada' literal here (shaping a snake out of clay/playdough). Direct and KB-CoT identical — injection had zero effect.

cs_sentence: Jakmile vystřelil po bažantovi, hodil flintu do žita a běžel ho hledat do pole.
injected_meaning: to give up; abandon an effort prematurely
direct_output: As soon as he shot at a pheasant, he threw the rifle into the wheat and ran to look for him in the field.
kbcot_output: As soon as he shot at a pheasant, he threw the rifle into the wheat and ran to look for him in the field.
note: HARDEST CASE: uses the idiom's core verb in a plausible literal hunting scene. Direct and KB-CoT identical — model held the literal reading despite the injection. NOTE: 'zito' mistranslated as 'wheat' rather than 'rye' in both conditions — separate, minor lexical error unrelated to the idiom test.

cs_sentence: Neházej tu zeleninu do jednoho pytle, ať to můžeme zvážit u pokladny.
injected_meaning: to treat distinct things or people as if they were all the same; to lump together
direct_output: Don't put all the vegetables in one bag so we can weigh them at the cash register.
kbcot_output: Don't put all the vegetables in one bag so we can weigh them at the checkout.
note: 'hazet do jednoho pytle' literal here (grocery shopping). Only difference is 'cash register' vs 'checkout' — cosmetic, not idiomatic drift.

cs_sentence: Tu kulku má blízko srdci, málem ho trefila.
injected_meaning: to hold something dear; care deeply about
direct_output: He has the bullet close to his heart, it almost hit him.
kbcot_output: He holds that bullet dear; it almost hit him.
note: srdce literal/anatomical (a bullet lodged near the heart, injury context). NOTE: KB-CoT ('holds that bullet dear') clearly drifted toward the figurative injected meaning without tripping the exact risk_phrases substring ('dear' alone wasn't listed) — a likely false negative in the automated flag; recommend treating this as effectively FLAGGED on manual review.
